# pr-minder

> pr-minder is a GitHub App (running as a Cloudflare Worker) that keeps pull requests healthy with zero per-repo workflow files. It keeps PR branches up to date with their base, syncs labels with GitHub's native auto-merge, revives "zombie" PRs whose CI never ran, and opens PRs for forgotten branches. It is opt-in and event-driven — no cron and no polling; it keeps only a little dedup state (which PRs it has already checked) in Workers KV.

## Install

Install the GitHub App here: https://github.com/apps/pr-minder/installations/new

Pick "All repositories" or a selected set. pr-minder does nothing until you add a config file, so installing it is safe — every behavior is opt-in.

## How it works

pr-minder receives GitHub webhooks (`pull_request`, `pull_request_review`, `push`, `installation`, `installation_repositories`). For each event it verifies the signature, mints a short-lived installation token, loads your config, and takes the configured actions through the GitHub REST and GraphQL APIs. There is no stored state.

Because it acts with the App's own credentials (not a workflow's default `GITHUB_TOKEN`), the PRs it creates and reopens trigger their workflows normally — which is the basis of the zombie-PR features below.

## Configuration

Config files are JSONC — `//` line comments and `/* */` block comments are allowed.

pr-minder resolves config in this order (first match wins):

1. `<repo>/.github/pr-minder.jsonc` — per-repo config (highest priority).
2. `<org>/.github` repo, at `.github/config/pr-minder/pr-minder.jsonc` — org-wide defaults. Per-repo overrides go under `repos.<repo-name>`; top-level keys are the org default.
3. No config found — everything is disabled.

Add the schema for editor validation and autocomplete:

```jsonc
{
  "$schema": "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/wow-look-at-my/pr-minder/master/schema/pr-minder.schema.json"
}
```

There is no global "enabled" switch. A feature is on only when its section is present and configured; "disable" means removing the section (or setting `auto_open_pr.enabled` / `auto_trigger_workflows` to `false`, or leaving `auto_update_pr.triggers` empty).

### auto_update_pr — keep PR branches up to date

`auto_update_pr.triggers` is a list of condition objects. When **any** condition is satisfied, pr-minder updates the PR branch with its base (GitHub's "Update branch"). Keys **within** one condition are ANDed; multiple conditions in the array are ORed. Updating is re-evaluated on PR events, on approving reviews, and on every push to the default branch.

| Key | Meaning |
|-----|---------|
| `label` | PR has this label. |
| `approved_by` | PR is approved by at least one of these usernames. |
| `min_approvals` | PR has at least this many approvals (from anyone). |

```jsonc
{
  "auto_update_pr": {
    "triggers": [
      { "label": "automerge" },
      { "approved_by": ["alice", "bob"], "min_approvals": 2 }
    ]
  }
}
```

### auto_label_pr — labels, auto-update, and auto-merge

A map of label name to per-label settings:

| Field | Meaning |
|-------|---------|
| `auto_add` | `"on_pr_creation"` applies the label when a PR is opened; `false` (default) disables. |
| `create_label_if_missing_in_repo` | `true` creates the label in the repo (using `color`) if it doesn't exist. |
| `color` | 6-digit hex, with or without a leading `#` (default `00FF00`). |
| `mode` | `"auto_update"`: keep the PR branch in sync with base whenever this label is present. `"auto_merge"`: bidirectionally sync this label with GitHub's native auto-merge. |
| `auto_merge_method` | `"merge"`, `"squash"` (default), or `"rebase"`. Only used with `mode: "auto_merge"`. |

With `mode: "auto_merge"`, adding the label arms GitHub's native auto-merge and removing it disarms it; toggling auto-merge from the GitHub UI adds/removes the label to match. Whenever GitHub won't arm native auto-merge, pr-minder merges the PR directly using `auto_merge_method` — this covers both an already-mergeable PR (GitHub refuses auto-merge on a "clean" PR) and a repo that hasn't enabled "Allow auto-merge" in its settings, so the label still merges your PRs there. Branch protection still gates every merge, and a PR that isn't mergeable yet is left untouched. (For the "wait for checks, then merge" behavior in a repo without native auto-merge, enable "Allow auto-merge" in the repo's Settings; pr-minder uses native auto-merge whenever the repo allows it.)

```jsonc
{
  "auto_label_pr": {
    "automerge": { "mode": "auto_merge", "auto_merge_method": "squash", "create_label_if_missing_in_repo": true, "color": "0e8a16" },
    "auto-update": { "mode": "auto_update", "auto_add": "on_pr_creation", "create_label_if_missing_in_repo": true }
  }
}
```

### auto_trigger_workflows — revive "zombie" PRs

`auto_trigger_workflows` is a boolean (default `false`).

A PR created with a workflow's default `GITHUB_TOKEN` (author `github-actions[bot]`) never triggers its own workflows — GitHub suppresses those runs to prevent recursion — so it sits open with no CI and can never go green. pr-minder revives these by closing and reopening the PR with its own App credentials, which fires a fresh event that runs the workflows for real. It only ever acts on PRs authored by `github-actions[bot]` (the kind that can be a `GITHUB_TOKEN` zombie).

It checks each PR **at most once per commit**, remembering what it has seen in Workers KV (keyed by the PR's head commit): a PR is only evaluated when its current commit hasn't been checked before, so an untouched PR is never re-checked, while new commits (a "touched" PR) are. When does it check?

- **Live events** — a PR being opened, reopened, or pushed to (`synchronize`) is checked immediately.
- **First time it sees a repo** — on the first webhook of any kind from a repo, it checks that repo's existing open PRs once. This is what revives zombies in repos that were *already* installed before the feature existed (GitHub never re-sends their install event), with no cron, no polling, and no reinstall — the repo's next bit of activity heals it.
- **On install / adding repos** — it checks the open PRs in the repos being added.

It will not loop: pr-minder's own reopen comes back as a `reopened` event sent by a bot, which is skipped.

```jsonc
{ "auto_trigger_workflows": true }
```

### auto_open_pr — open PRs for forgotten branches

`auto_open_pr` opens a PR for any branch that is ahead of its base and has no open PR, **using pr-minder's App credentials** so the PR triggers its workflows normally (it is never a zombie). This is the root-cause fix for the "a CI job opened a PR with `GITHUB_TOKEN` and CI never ran" problem.

| Field | Meaning |
|-------|---------|
| `enabled` | Turn the feature on (default `false`). |
| `skip_branches` | Extra branch names to never open PRs for. The default branch and `gh-pages` are always skipped. |
| `target_base` | Base branch for opened PRs (default: the repo's default branch). |

It runs on a push to a non-default branch (opening the PR as soon as the branch gets a commit) and sweeps existing branches once when the App is installed or repositories are added. Fork branches are not opened.

```jsonc
{
  "auto_open_pr": {
    "enabled": true,
    "skip_branches": ["staging"],
    "target_base": ""
  }
}
```

## Org-wide config with per-repo overrides

In the `<org>/.github` repo at `.github/config/pr-minder/pr-minder.jsonc`:

```jsonc
{
  "$schema": "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/wow-look-at-my/pr-minder/master/schema/pr-minder.schema.json",
  "auto_update_pr": { "triggers": [{ "label": "automerge" }] },
  "auto_trigger_workflows": true,
  "repos": {
    "special-repo": {
      "auto_update_pr": { "triggers": [{ "label": "ready-to-merge" }] }
    },
    "opt-out-repo": {
      "auto_update_pr": { "triggers": [] }
    }
  }
}
```

In overrides, `auto_update_pr` replaces the parent's entirely; `auto_label_pr` and `auto_open_pr` merge field-by-field.

## Links

- Install: https://github.com/apps/pr-minder/installations/new
- Source code: https://github.com/wow-look-at-my/pr-minder
- Config JSON Schema: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/wow-look-at-my/pr-minder/master/schema/pr-minder.schema.json
